title: COS101 - Introduction to Computing
description: Fundamental concepts of computing including computer systems, input and output devices, storage, and basic IT principles.

topics.title: Characteristics of a Computer
topics.description: The key attributes that define how a computer operates, including speed, accuracy, storage, automation, reliability, and flexibility.

questions.question: Which characteristic of a computer allows it to process large volumes of data at incredible speed?
questions.explanation: Speed is the characteristic that allows computers to manipulate large volumes of data at incredible response times.

questions.question: Computer accuracy means that:
options: Results are consistently correct with minimal error
questions.explanation: Accuracy means the computer has great consistency and reliability in its results; most errors arise from human input, not the machine.

questions.question: Errors in computer output are mostly caused by:
questions.explanation: Computers have built-in error-detecting schemes; most computational errors trace back to human mistakes in programming or data entry.

questions.question: Memory capacity in early computers was built up in K (Kilo) modules where K equals:
questions.explanation: 1K = 1024 memory locations, which is the standard binary-based unit used to measure computer memory.

questions.question: A computer running a program with little or no human involvement demonstrates which characteristic?
questions.explanation: The Automatic characteristic means a program stored in memory can run on its own each time it is executed with minimal human intervention.

questions.question: Which computer characteristic ensures it performs every job with the same speed and accuracy without fatigue?
questions.explanation: Reliability means the computer, as a machine, does not suffer human traits such as tiredness or loss of concentration, so it delivers consistent performance.

questions.question: Which characteristic allows a computer to perform tasks like online processing, multi-programming, and real-time processing?
questions.explanation: Flexibility means a computer can do any type of job or task, as long as it can be reduced to logical steps, including diverse modes of processing.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a basic characteristic of a computer?
questions.explanation: Computers do not have emotions. The core characteristics include speed, accuracy, storage, automation, reliability, and flexibility.

questions.question: A computer that consistently produces the same output for the same input, regardless of how many times it runs, is demonstrating:
questions.explanation: Reliability refers to the computer's ability to perform every job consistently with the same accuracy and speed, unlike humans who can be inconsistent.

questions.question: Which characteristic of a computer is being demonstrated when it executes a payroll program every month without re-programming?
questions.explanation: The Automatic characteristic means a stored program can run automatically each time it is executed without requiring human re-intervention.

questions.question: Computer storage capacity is measured in units of:
questions.explanation: Storage is measured in K (Kilo) modules where 1K = 1024 memory locations, with higher units including Mega, Giga, and Terabytes.

questions.question: Which pair of computer characteristics directly ensures that users can trust the results of computation?
questions.explanation: Accuracy ensures results are correct, and Reliability ensures the computer performs consistently every time, together making results trustworthy.

questions.question: A hospital uses a computer to automatically generate daily patient reports without staff intervention. This best illustrates:
questions.explanation: Automatic operation means once a program is loaded, it runs with little or no human involvement, as in automated report generation.

questions.question: Which characteristic allows a single computer to be used for tasks as varied as video editing, database management, and word processing?
questions.explanation: Flexibility enables a computer to perform any type of task that can be expressed as logical steps, making it adaptable to varied uses.

questions.question: In-built error-detecting schemes in a computer are related to which characteristic?
questions.explanation: Accuracy includes the computer's in-built error-detecting capabilities that help ensure output correctness.

questions.question: A computer performing the same data processing task for 24 hours without degradation in performance illustrates:
questions.explanation: Reliability means the computer does not experience fatigue or diminishing returns, maintaining consistent performance over extended periods.

questions.question: Which characteristic of a computer allows it to store both data and instructions for later use?
questions.explanation: Storage is the characteristic that provides internal and external facilities for holding data and instructions, with capacity varying by machine.

questions.question: A computer used in a factory to automatically control machinery based on sensor readings demonstrates:
questions.explanation: Such a system demonstrates Automatic operation (runs without human input) and Flexibility (adapts to different tasks/conditions).

questions.question: Which characteristic of a computer makes it suitable for real-time processing applications like air traffic control?
questions.explanation: Real-time control demands Speed (instant processing) and Reliability (consistent, failure-free performance) to ensure safety.

questions.question: Why is human error considered the primary source of computer inaccuracies?
options: Computers execute instructions exactly as programmed
questions.explanation: Computers faithfully execute whatever instructions are given. If those instructions or input data contain mistakes, the output will be incorrect — hence the term 'garbage in, garbage out.'

questions.question: A computer storing a student's academic record for future retrieval demonstrates which characteristic?
questions.explanation: Storage is the characteristic that allows computers to hold data and instructions both internally and externally for future use.

questions.question: Which of the following best compares a computer to a human worker?
options: A computer is more reliable because it does not experience fatigue
A human worker is more accurate
A human is faster at calculations
questions.explanation: Unlike humans, computers do not suffer from fatigue or loss of concentration, making them more reliable for repetitive tasks.

questions.question: Six characteristics of a computer are often summarized as:
options: Speed, Size, Memory, Cost, Age, Weight
Speed, Accuracy, Storage, Automatic, Reliability, Flexibility
Input, Output, Process, Store, Control, Display
Hardware, Software, Network, Database, User, Interface
questions.explanation: The six key characteristics are Speed, Accuracy, Storage, Automatic, Reliability, and Flexibility — commonly taught in introductory computing courses.

questions.question: A computer can perform millions of calculations per second. This refers to its:
questions.explanation: Speed is measured in operations per second, and modern computers can perform millions or billions of operations each second.

questions.question: When a computer uses stored data from a previous session to auto-fill a form, it demonstrates:
questions.explanation: Storage provides the saved data, and the Automatic characteristic enables its use without re-entry by the user.

topics.title: The Computing System
topics.description: Understanding the components of a computing system including hardware, software, users, and the computing environment.

questions.question: A computing system is made up of:
questions.explanation: The computing system includes the computer system itself, the user who operates it, and the environment in which it is used.

questions.question: The computer system itself consists of:
questions.explanation: The computer system is made up of both hardware (physical components) and software (instructions and programs).

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT part of the computing system?
questions.explanation: The computing system consists of the computer system, the user, and the environment. An electricity bill is not a component of the system itself.

questions.question: The hardware unit that accepts data fed into the computer is called:
questions.explanation: The input unit comprises all media and devices through which data is fed into the computer, such as keyboards and mice.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an input device?
questions.explanation: A keyboard is an input device used to enter data into the computer. Monitors, printers, and speakers are output devices.

questions.question: The processing unit of a computer is made up of:
options: ALU, Control Unit, and main memory
questions.explanation: The processing unit includes the Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU), the Control Unit, and the main memory (RAM and ROM).

questions.question: The main memory of a computer, also known as primary memory, consists of:
questions.explanation: Primary (main) memory is made up of ROM (Read Only Memory) and RAM (Random Access Memory).

questions.question: Which unit of the computer displays results and information to the user?
questions.explanation: The output unit contains devices like monitors and printers that display processed data and results to the user.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an output device?
questions.explanation: A monitor (Visual Display Unit) is an output device. Scanners, keyboards, and mice are input devices.

questions.question: The 'brain' of a computer system is the:
questions.explanation: The CPU (Central Processing Unit) is referred to as the brain of the computer because it performs all processing operations.

questions.question: ALU stands for:
questions.explanation: ALU stands for Arithmetic Logic Unit, the part of the CPU that executes arithmetic operations (add, subtract) and logical comparisons.

questions.question: Computer software can be categorized into:
questions.explanation: Computer software is broadly divided into system software (manages hardware, provides platform) and application software (user-oriented tasks).

questions.question: A program is best defined as:
options: A group of instructions put together to perform a task
questions.explanation: A program is a combination of instructions grouped together to perform a specific task; multiple programs linked together form software.

questions.question: Expert users in a computing system include:
questions.explanation: Expert users are those with deep technical knowledge: computer engineers (build systems), programmers (write code), and operators (run the systems).

questions.question: Casual users of computers are those who:
options: Use computers occasionally without deep technical knowledge
questions.explanation: Casual users are the general public who use computers for everyday tasks without in-depth technical training.

questions.question: The computing environment includes:
options: Building, furniture, UPS, fans, and air conditioners
questions.explanation: The computing environment encompasses the physical space (building, furniture) and auxiliary devices like UPS, voltage stabilizers, fans, and air conditioners.

questions.question: Which of the following is a function of system software?
options: Managing computer hardware and providing a platform for applications
questions.explanation: System software manages the hardware and provides a platform on which application software can run. Examples include operating systems.

questions.question: Microsoft Word is an example of:
questions.explanation: Microsoft Word is application software designed to help users accomplish a specific task — word processing.

questions.question: Which of the following is utility software?
questions.explanation: Utility software performs system maintenance tasks. A disk formatter is a utility tool, while Excel, Chrome, and Photoshop are application software.

questions.question: The functional parts of a computer system include:
options: Input unit, processing unit, and output unit
questions.explanation: The functional parts of a computer system are the input unit (data entry), processing unit (computation), and output unit (results display).

questions.question: Which component of the computing system ensures the computer has adequate power and cooling?
questions.explanation: The computing environment includes auxiliary devices such as UPS, voltage stabilizers, and air conditioners that support stable operation.

questions.question: A series of programs linked together make up:
questions.explanation: Multiple programs linked together form software. A program itself is a group of instructions, and software is a collection of such programs.

questions.question: Which part of the CPU directs data flow between the CPU and other components?
questions.explanation: The Control Unit directs and coordinates data flow between the CPU and other parts of the computer system.

questions.question: Device drivers are a type of:
questions.explanation: Device drivers are system software that allow hardware components to communicate properly with the operating system.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between hardware and software?
options: Hardware and software work together to make a computer functional
questions.explanation: Hardware provides the physical infrastructure, while software provides the instructions. Both are necessary for a functional computer system.

topics.title: Applications of Computers in Various Fields
topics.description: How computers are applied across sectors including business, banking, education, healthcare, engineering, military, communication, and government.

questions.question: Computers are used in business organizations mainly for:
options: Payroll calculations, budgeting, and sales analysis
questions.explanation: In business, computers handle payroll, budgeting, sales analysis, employee database management, and inventory, making operations more efficient.

questions.question: ATM machines in banking are described as:
questions.explanation: ATMs are completely automated, allowing customers to perform transactions such as withdrawals and balance inquiries without a human teller.

questions.question: Which banking facility allows customers to check current balances, make deposits, and check interest charges online?
questions.explanation: Online accounting facilities (internet banking) allow customers to check balances, make deposits, monitor interest, and manage accounts remotely.

questions.question: Insurance companies use computers mainly to:
options: Keep records up-to-date and manage client databases
questions.explanation: Insurance companies use computers to maintain client databases, track policy details, premium installments, maturity dates, and bonus information.

questions.question: CBE in education stands for:
questions.explanation: CBE stands for Computer Based Education, which involves the control, delivery, and evaluation of learning using computers.

questions.question: In the field of education, computers are used to:
options: Prepare student performance databases and support e-learning
questions.explanation: Computers in education support CBE, maintain student performance databases, deliver e-learning content, and analyze academic data.

questions.question: In marketing, computers are used for:
options: Advertising, home shopping, and market analysis
questions.explanation: Computers in marketing help advertising professionals create graphics and ads, enable home shopping via computerized catalogues, and support market analysis.

questions.question: Which healthcare application uses computerized machines to scan and diagnose diseases?
questions.explanation: ECG, EEG, CT scans, and ultrasounds are computerized diagnostic tools used in healthcare to detect and diagnose medical conditions.

questions.question: The Diagnostic System in healthcare refers to computers being used to:
options: Collect patient records and identify the cause of illness
questions.explanation: Diagnostic Systems collect patient data and help identify the cause of illness, making diagnosis faster and more accurate.

questions.question: Patient Monitoring Systems using computers are mainly used to:
options: Check patients' vital signs for abnormalities such as in Cardiac Arrest
questions.explanation: Patient Monitoring Systems track vital signs in real time, alerting medical staff to abnormalities like irregular heartbeat or cardiac arrest.

questions.question: CAD in engineering design stands for:
questions.explanation: CAD stands for Computer Aided Design, a technology used to create and modify engineering and architectural images and designs.

questions.question: Which type of engineering uses computers for stress and strain analysis in ships and buildings?
questions.explanation: Structural Engineering uses computers to analyze stress and strain in large structures such as ships, buildings, and airplanes.

questions.question: In the military, computers are used for:
options: Missile control, military communication, and smart weapons
questions.explanation: Military uses of computers include missile control systems, military communication networks, operation planning, and smart weapons technology.

questions.question: Which of the following is a communication application of computers?
questions.explanation: Communication applications include e-mail, chatting, Usenet, FTP, Telnet, and video-conferencing — all enabled by computer networks.

questions.question: In government, computers are used for:
options: Budgeting, tax processing, voter registration, and weather forecasting
questions.explanation: Government uses computers for budgets, tax departments, voter list computerization, PAN card systems, and weather forecasting.

questions.question: Computers in hospitals are used to:
options: Keep patient records and conduct diagnostic imaging
questions.explanation: Hospitals use computers to maintain patient and medicine records, conduct ECGs, CT scans, and manage lab diagnostic systems.

questions.question: FTP in computer communication stands for:
questions.explanation: FTP stands for File Transfer Protocol, used to transfer files between computers over a network.

questions.question: Home shopping has been made possible through:
options: Computerized catalogues that allow direct order entry
questions.explanation: Computerized catalogues accessible via the internet allow customers to browse products, access information, and place orders from home.

questions.question: Architectural Engineering uses computers to:
options: Plan towns, design buildings, and create 2D and 3D drawings
questions.explanation: Architectural Engineering leverages computers and CAD to plan urban areas, design buildings, and create detailed 2D and 3D representations.

questions.question: Which application area uses computers to maintain databases of voter lists and PAN cards?
questions.explanation: Government uses computers to computerize voters' lists, PAN cards, and other public records for better administration and transparency.

questions.question: Pharma Information Systems in healthcare are used to:
options: Check drug labels, expiry dates, and harmful side effects
questions.explanation: Pharma Information Systems help pharmacists and doctors check drug labels, monitor expiry dates, and assess potential side effects.

questions.question: The use of computers in advertising helps professionals to:
options: Create art, write copy, and disseminate ads
questions.explanation: Computers allow advertising professionals to create graphics, draft and revise copy, and distribute advertisements digitally to sell products.

questions.question: Industrial Engineering uses computers for:
options: Design and improvement of integrated systems of people, materials, and equipment
questions.explanation: Industrial Engineering uses computers to design, implement, and improve integrated manufacturing and operational systems.

questions.question: Which is a correct example of computer use in banking?
options: Electronic funds transfer and ATM operations
questions.explanation: Banking relies on computers for electronic fund transfers, ATM operations, online account management, and customer record keeping.

questions.question: Insurance companies use computers to track which of the following policy details?
options: Starting date, maturity date, premium installments, interests, and survival benefits
questions.explanation: Insurance companies maintain comprehensive policy databases covering start dates, maturity dates, premium schedules, interest, bonus, and survival benefits.

topics.title: Generations of Computers
topics.description: The five generations of computers, their defining technologies, key features, and representative examples.

questions.question: How many generations of computers are known to date?
questions.explanation: There are five recognized computer generations, each defined by the core technology used: vacuum tubes, transistors, ICs, VLSI, and ULSI/AI.

questions.question: First generation computers used which core technology?
questions.explanation: First generation computers (1940–1956) used vacuum tubes as their basic electronic component.

questions.question: Which language did first generation computers support?
questions.explanation: First generation computers only supported machine language (binary code), which was difficult and tedious to program.

questions.question: ENIAC is an example of which generation of computers?
questions.explanation: ENIAC (Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer) is a first-generation computer that used vacuum tube technology.

questions.question: Second generation computers used which technology?
questions.explanation: Second generation computers (1959–1965) replaced vacuum tubes with transistors, which were smaller, cheaper, and more reliable.

questions.question: Which programming languages were introduced in the second generation?
questions.explanation: Second generation computers saw the introduction of high-level languages like FORTRAN (for science) and COBOL (for business).

questions.question: Third generation computers used:
questions.explanation: Third generation computers (1965–1971) used Integrated Circuits (ICs), which packed many transistors and resistors onto a single chip.

questions.question: Who invented the Integrated Circuit (IC)?
questions.explanation: Jack Kilby invented the Integrated Circuit, which became the defining technology of third generation computers.

questions.question: Fourth generation computers used which technology?
questions.explanation: Fourth generation computers (1971–1980) used Very Large Scale Integrated (VLSI) circuits, enabling microcomputers and personal computers.

questions.question: Personal Computers (PCs) became common in which generation?
questions.explanation: The fourth generation saw the PC revolution, as VLSI technology enabled affordable, compact personal computers for home and office use.

questions.question: Fifth generation computers are characterized by:
questions.explanation: Fifth generation computers use Ultra Large Scale Integration (ULSI) and incorporate Artificial Intelligence (AI) and parallel processing.

questions.question: VLSI circuits in the fourth generation had approximately how many transistors per chip?
questions.explanation: VLSI circuits had about 5000 transistors and other circuit elements on a single chip, enabling powerful but compact computers.

questions.question: Which generation introduced the concept of the internet?
questions.explanation: The fourth generation saw the introduction of the concept of the internet, along with great developments in the fields of networks.

questions.question: Which operating system concepts were used in second generation computers?
questions.explanation: Second generation computers used batch processing (jobs processed in batches) and multiprogramming operating systems.

questions.question: Third generation computers introduced which mode of processing?
questions.explanation: Third generation computers introduced remote processing, time-sharing (multiple users simultaneously), and multi-programming.

questions.question: Which of the following is a feature of first generation computers?
options: Huge size, unreliable, and generates large heat
questions.explanation: First generation computers were enormous, unreliable, generated large amounts of heat, were very costly, and only supported machine language.

questions.question: The IBM-360 series is an example of which computer generation?
questions.explanation: The IBM-360 series are third generation computers that used Integrated Circuits and supported high-level programming languages.

questions.question: What does ULSI stand for?
questions.explanation: ULSI stands for Ultra Large Scale Integration, used in fifth generation computers, enabling microprocessor chips with ten million or more components.

questions.question: Fifth generation computers support which high-level languages?
questions.explanation: Fifth generation computers support modern high-level languages including C, C++, Java, .Net, and many others.

questions.question: Which feature is unique to fifth generation computers compared to earlier generations?
questions.explanation: Fifth generation computers are distinguished by AI capabilities, parallel processing, and natural language processing — not present in earlier generations.

questions.question: The second generation covered the period:
questions.explanation: Second generation computers were dominant from 1959 to 1965, characterized by transistor technology.

questions.question: Which storage media was used as secondary storage in second generation computers?
questions.explanation: Second generation computers used magnetic tape and magnetic disks for secondary storage, with magnetic cores as primary memory.

questions.question: Portable laptop and notebook computers belong to which generation?
questions.explanation: Laptops, notebooks, ultrabooks, and chromebooks are all fifth generation computer types that emerged from the ULSI era.

questions.question: Which generation required air conditioning to operate?
questions.explanation: First, second, and third generation computers required air conditioning (AC) due to heat generation. Fourth generation computers did not require AC.

questions.question: UNIVAC is associated with which computer generation?
questions.explanation: UNIVAC (Universal Automatic Computer) is a first-generation computer that used vacuum tube technology.

topics.title: Computer Hardware: CPU, Memory, and Storage
topics.description: Detailed coverage of CPU components, primary memory (RAM and ROM), and secondary storage devices.

questions.question: What does CPU stand for?
questions.explanation: CPU stands for Central Processing Unit, the primary component that performs all computation and processing in a computer.

questions.question: Which component of the CPU performs arithmetic calculations like addition and subtraction?
questions.explanation: The Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) executes arithmetic operations such as addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division, as well as logical comparisons.

questions.question: RAM stands for:
questions.explanation: RAM stands for Random Access Memory, the primary working memory of a computer that stores data temporarily during processing.

questions.question: RAM is described as volatile because:
options: Data is lost when power is switched off
questions.explanation: RAM is volatile, meaning all data stored in it is lost when the power supply is turned off. This makes it suitable only for temporary storage.

questions.question: ROM stands for:
questions.explanation: ROM stands for Read Only Memory, a non-volatile storage that retains data permanently even when power is off.

questions.question: Which memory remains active regardless of whether power is on or off?
questions.explanation: ROM (Read Only Memory) is non-volatile — it retains its stored data permanently, even when the computer is powered off.

questions.question: Primary memory (main memory) consists of:
questions.explanation: Primary memory is made up of RAM (temporary, volatile) and ROM (permanent, non-volatile), both directly accessible by the CPU.

questions.question: Which type of secondary storage uses rotating magnetic platters?
questions.explanation: A Hard Disk Drive (HDD) uses electromagnetically charged rotating platters to store large amounts of data permanently.

questions.question: Optical disk drives use which technology to read and write data?
questions.explanation: Optical disk drives use laser light as part of the process of reading and writing data from CDs, DVDs, and Blu-ray discs.

questions.question: A flash disk stores data using:
questions.explanation: Flash disks use flash memory chips — solid-state technology with no moving parts — to store data electronically.

questions.question: Which type of memory is the fastest?
questions.explanation: Cache memory is the fastest memory type, located inside or near the CPU to store frequently accessed data for rapid retrieval.

questions.question: Registers in the CPU are used for:
options: Holding data currently being processed by the CPU
questions.explanation: Registers are tiny, ultra-fast storage locations inside the CPU that hold data and instructions currently being executed.

questions.question: The main difference between RAM and ROM is:
options: RAM is volatile while ROM is non-volatile
RAM is permanent while ROM is temporary
questions.explanation: RAM is volatile (loses data when power off) and used for active processing, while ROM is non-volatile (retains data permanently) and stores firmware.

questions.question: BIOS is stored in:
questions.explanation: BIOS (Basic Input Output System) is firmware stored in ROM, enabling the computer to boot and initialize hardware during startup.

questions.question: The motherboard is best described as:
options: The main circuit board connecting all computer components
questions.explanation: The motherboard is the main circuit board (backbone) of the computer, connecting the CPU, RAM, storage, and all other components.

questions.question: PSU in computer hardware stands for:
questions.explanation: PSU stands for Power Supply Unit, which converts AC power from the wall outlet into DC power for internal computer components.

questions.question: SSD stands for:
questions.explanation: SSD stands for Solid State Drive, a storage device with no moving mechanical parts, making it faster and more durable than HDDs.

questions.question: Which component is directly responsible for video output quality?
questions.explanation: The graphics card (GPU) processes and renders images and video, directly affecting the quality of visual output on the monitor.

questions.question: NIC stands for:
questions.explanation: NIC stands for Network Interface Card, the hardware component that enables a computer to connect to a network.

questions.question: CMOS battery in a computer is used to:
options: Retain BIOS settings when power is off
questions.explanation: The CMOS battery maintains BIOS settings (including date and time) even when the computer is unplugged or powered off.

questions.question: Which internal port is commonly used to connect storage drives like HDD and SSD internally?
questions.explanation: SATA (Serial ATA) is the standard internal port used to connect hard drives and SSDs to the motherboard.

questions.question: The Control Unit in the CPU is responsible for:
options: Managing data flow and coordinating CPU activities
questions.explanation: The Control Unit directs the flow of data between the CPU and other components, coordinating all processing activities.

questions.question: Cache memory is used to:
options: Speed up processing by holding frequently used data close to the CPU
questions.explanation: Cache memory temporarily stores frequently accessed data and instructions close to the CPU, significantly speeding up processing.

questions.question: Which memory type is also called auxiliary memory or secondary memory?
questions.explanation: Hard disk (HDD) is called auxiliary or secondary memory because it stores data permanently and is not directly accessed by the CPU during processing.

questions.question: The RAM size of a computer typically ranges from:
options: 500 MB to 6 GB (standard range noted in curriculum)
questions.explanation: According to the curriculum document, RAM typically ranges from 500 MB to 6 GB for standard computing, though modern computers may have more.

topics.title: Input Devices
topics.description: Classification and detailed study of input devices including keyboard, mouse, joystick, light pen, touch screen, scanners, and special devices like MICR, OMR, OCR.

questions.question: What is an input device?
options: A device that stores data permanently
A peripheral used to enter data into a computer system
questions.explanation: An input device is any peripheral that converts human-readable data or actions into a form the computer can process.

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct function of input devices?
options: Accept data from the outside world and convert it to binary code
questions.explanation: Input devices accept data from the external world, convert it into computer-coded (binary) information, and supply it to the CPU.

questions.question: The most commonly used input device is the:
questions.explanation: The keyboard is the most common standard input device, used to enter alphanumeric characters, special symbols, and control commands.

questions.question: The most popular general-purpose keyboard contains how many keys?
questions.explanation: The most popular general-purpose keyboard contains 101 keys (though 104-key versions are also common in modern PCs).

questions.question: The alphanumeric keypad on a keyboard is located:
questions.explanation: The alphanumeric keypad is the central part of the keyboard containing alphabets, numbers, and special symbols like !, @, #, $.

questions.question: The numeric keypad is located on which side of the keyboard?
questions.explanation: The numeric keypad is on the rightmost section of the keyboard, containing digits 0-9 and mathematical operators (+, -, *, /).

questions.question: Function keys on a keyboard are labeled:
questions.explanation: Function keys are labeled F1 through F12 and are located in the top row of the keyboard. They generate shortcuts in various software.

questions.question: The BACKSPACE key is used to:
options: Delete character to the right of cursor
Delete character to the left of cursor
questions.explanation: The BACKSPACE key removes the character immediately to the left of the cursor position.

questions.question: The DELETE key is used to:
options: Delete character to the right of the cursor
Delete character to the left of the cursor
questions.explanation: The DELETE key removes the character immediately to the right of the cursor position, unlike BACKSPACE which removes to the left.

questions.question: The HOME key moves the cursor to:
questions.explanation: The HOME key moves the cursor to the beginning of the current line.

questions.question: The ENTER key is used to:
options: Move cursor to the next defined position
Execute commands and move to next line
questions.explanation: The ENTER key executes commands, confirms inputs, and in word processors it moves the cursor to the beginning of a new line.

questions.question: CAPSLOCK is used to:
questions.explanation: CAPSLOCK toggles the keyboard to type all alphabetic characters in uppercase without holding the SHIFT key.

questions.question: A special-purpose keyboard differs from a general-purpose keyboard in that it:
options: Has fewer keys designed for specific tasks
questions.explanation: Special-purpose keyboards have fewer, task-specific keys designed for rapid interaction in particular environments, like ATM keypads.

questions.question: A mouse is classified as:
questions.explanation: A mouse is a pointing device that controls an on-screen cursor by detecting motion on a flat surface.

questions.question: What action is performed when you press and release the left mouse button twice rapidly?
questions.explanation: Double-clicking involves pressing and releasing the left mouse button twice in quick succession, typically used to open applications or files.

questions.question: The DRAG mouse operation is used to:
options: Move objects on the screen by holding the left button while moving
questions.explanation: Dragging involves holding the left mouse button down while moving the mouse to relocate objects or select regions on the screen.

questions.question: An advantage of a trackball over a mouse is that it:
options: Requires less desk space and less wrist strain
questions.explanation: Trackballs require less desk space, reduce arm and wrist strain, and do not need a mouse pad since the user rolls the ball with their fingers.

questions.question: A joystick is different from a mouse because:
options: The cursor continues moving in the direction the joystick points until it is returned upright
A joystick cannot be used for games
questions.explanation: Unlike a mouse (which stops moving when stationary), a joystick keeps the cursor moving as long as it is tilted away from the center position.

questions.question: Joysticks are widely used in:
questions.explanation: Joysticks are commonly used in video games, flight simulators, aircraft, UAVs, motorized wheelchairs, and other control systems.

questions.question: A light pen is useful for:
options: Drawing directly on a screen, especially in CAD
questions.explanation: A light pen is a pointing device that allows users to draw or select points directly on a CRT screen, ideal for CAD, graphics, and medical imaging.

questions.question: An advantage of a light pen over a touch screen is that it:
options: Cannot damage the screen and is less expensive
questions.explanation: A light pen is less expensive than a full touch screen, cannot scratch or damage the screen, and works on any screen size.

questions.question: A touch screen allows user interaction by:
options: Touching the screen directly with a finger or stylus
questions.explanation: Touch screens detect the presence and location of a touch on the display, allowing direct interaction by touching icons and menu items.

questions.question: Touch screens are commonly found in which places?
options: Airports, ATMs, departmental stores, smartphones, and museums
questions.explanation: Touch screens are widely used in airports, railway stations, ATMs, department stores, museums, smartphones, and kiosks.

questions.question: A digitizer is used to:
options: Convert drawings, maps, and images into digital form
questions.explanation: A digitizer converts hand-drawn images, maps, and pictures into digital form using a stylus on a flat tablet surface.

questions.question: MICR stands for:
questions.explanation: MICR stands for Magnetic Ink Character Recognition, a technology that reads magnetized characters printed on bank cheques.

questions.question: MICR is commonly used in:
questions.explanation: MICR is primarily used in the banking industry to process cheques quickly and accurately by reading magnetic ink characters.

questions.question: OMR stands for:
questions.explanation: OMR stands for Optical Mark Recognition, a technology that detects marks made in designated positions on specially designed forms.

questions.question: OMR is commonly used for:
options: Scanning exam answer sheets and survey forms
questions.explanation: OMR is widely used to evaluate multiple-choice answer sheets, voting forms, and survey questionnaires by detecting pencil or pen marks.

questions.question: OCR stands for:
questions.explanation: OCR stands for Optical Character Recognition, technology that converts scanned images of text into machine-readable ASCII code.

questions.question: A barcode reader uses which technology to read barcodes?
questions.explanation: A barcode reader uses a laser beam that scans the bar pattern; reflected light is decoded into electrical pulses and then into alphanumeric characters.

questions.question: UPC in barcode technology stands for:
questions.explanation: UPC stands for Universal Product Code, the standard barcode used on retail products, containing manufacturer and product information.

questions.question: In a UPC barcode, the first 5 digits identify:
questions.explanation: In UPC, the first 5 digits identify the manufacturer, and the last 5 digits identify the specific product.

questions.question: A scanner is mainly used to:
options: Input graphical data and typed documents into a computer
questions.explanation: Scanners are input devices that convert physical documents, photographs, and graphics into digital form for storage and processing.

questions.question: A flatbed scanner scans documents:
options: Line by line as the light source moves across the document
questions.explanation: A flatbed scanner has a glass plate where the document is placed; a light source moves horizontally, scanning the document line by line.

questions.question: A microphone converts:
questions.explanation: A microphone is a transducer that converts sound wave energy into electrical signals, which are then processed by the computer.

questions.question: A webcam is primarily used to:
options: Capture and transmit video over a network
questions.explanation: A webcam captures images and video, transmitting them over a network (via USB or Wi-Fi) for video conferencing, surveillance, or streaming.

questions.question: Webcam software commonly compresses captured images into:
questions.explanation: Webcam software compresses captured images into JPEG format to reduce file size for easier storage and transmission.

questions.question: Which device is used in supermarkets to read product codes at checkout?
questions.explanation: Barcode readers (BCR) are used at supermarket checkouts to scan product barcodes, retrieve prices, and process sales transactions.

questions.question: Which device is used in banks for reading cheques?
questions.explanation: MICR (Magnetic Ink Character Recognition) is specifically used in banking to read the magnetic characters printed on cheques.

questions.question: Input devices are classified into which main categories?
options: Standard input, pointing devices, and special input devices
questions.explanation: Input devices are classified as standard input devices (keyboard, mouse), pointing devices (trackball, joystick), and special input devices (MICR, BCR, scanner).

topics.title: Output Devices
topics.description: Study of output devices including monitors (CRT, LCD, Plasma), printers (impact and non-impact), plotters, and speakers.

questions.question: An output device is used to:
options: Display or present results from the computer
questions.explanation: Output devices convert processed data from the computer into a human-readable form such as visual display, printed documents, or audio.

questions.question: Which of the following is an output device?
questions.explanation: A monitor (Visual Display Unit) is an output device. Keyboards, mice, and scanners are input devices.

questions.question: CRT stands for:
questions.explanation: CRT stands for Cathode Ray Tube, the original monitor technology that uses an electron beam striking a phosphor-coated screen.

questions.question: In a CRT monitor, images are produced when electron beams strike:
questions.explanation: In CRT monitors, an electron gun fires beams that strike phosphor dots on the screen, causing them to glow and produce the visible image.

questions.question: CRT phosphor dots contain which three colors?
questions.explanation: CRT screens use red, green, and blue (RGB) phosphor dots. Varying the intensity of these three creates all visible colors.

questions.question: A disadvantage of CRT monitors is:
options: High power consumption and large size
questions.explanation: CRT monitors are heavy, bulky, and consume significantly more power than LCD or LED displays, making them less practical.

questions.question: LCD stands for:
questions.explanation: LCD stands for Liquid Crystal Display, a flat-panel technology that uses liquid crystals to modulate backlight.

questions.question: LCD screens do not emit their own:
questions.explanation: LCD panels do not self-illuminate; they modulate (control the passage of) backlight. This is why LCD screens require a backlight source.

questions.question: An advantage of LCD over CRT monitors is:
options: Flat screen and low power consumption
questions.explanation: LCDs are flat, lightweight, consume less electricity, and produce bright images — making them superior to bulky, power-hungry CRTs.

questions.question: A disadvantage of LCD screens is:
options: Fixed resolution and limited viewing angle
questions.explanation: LCD screens have a fixed native resolution and a limited viewing angle; image quality degrades when viewed from the side.

questions.question: Plasma display panels produce images using:
questions.explanation: Plasma displays use ionized gas (plasma) between two glass panels. The gas emits UV light when energized, activating phosphor dots to produce color.

questions.question: A printer produces which type of output?
questions.explanation: Printers produce hard copy output — a physical, tangible printout of data on paper or other media.

questions.question: Printers are broadly classified into:
questions.explanation: Printers are classified as impact (physically strike a ribbon) and non-impact (spray ink or use laser/toner without striking).

questions.question: Impact printers work by:
options: Striking an ink ribbon against paper
questions.explanation: Impact printers physically strike an ink ribbon against paper to transfer ink, similar to how a typewriter works.

questions.question: Which is an example of an impact printer?
questions.explanation: A dot matrix printer is an impact printer that uses a matrix of pins to strike an ink ribbon and create characters.

questions.question: One major advantage of impact printers is:
questions.explanation: Impact printers physically press ink onto paper, making them capable of producing multiple carbon copies simultaneously — useful in invoicing.

questions.question: Dot matrix printers can print approximately how many characters per second?
questions.explanation: Dot matrix printers operate at approximately 30–600 characters per second (cps), depending on the model and mode.

questions.question: Non-impact printers produce output by:
options: Spraying ink or fusing toner without physically striking paper
questions.explanation: Non-impact printers (inkjet, laser) transfer ink or toner to paper without physical contact, resulting in quieter, higher-quality output.

questions.question: Inkjet printers create images by:
options: Spraying microscopic ink droplets through nozzles
questions.explanation: Inkjet printers use microscopic nozzles to spray heated ink droplets onto paper, producing detailed images and text.

questions.question: Laser printers are fastest and produce the highest quality because they use:
questions.explanation: Laser printers use a laser beam to charge a drum, which attracts toner powder; heat and pressure then fix the toner to paper for crisp output.

questions.question: Which type of printer cannot produce carbon copies?
questions.explanation: Laser and other non-impact printers cannot produce carbon copies because they do not physically strike paper — unlike impact printers.

questions.question: A plotter is mainly used for:
options: Producing large-format graphic output using pens
questions.explanation: Plotters draw vector graphics on paper using pens, commonly used by engineers and architects for large-scale technical drawings.

questions.question: A drum plotter differs from a flatbed plotter in that:
options: Drum plotter uses a rotating drum while the pen moves linearly
Drum plotter is used for audio
questions.explanation: In a drum plotter, the paper wraps around a rotating drum while the pen moves back and forth; in a flatbed plotter, the paper is stationary and the pen moves in X-Y axes.

questions.question: Speakers convert digital audio signals into:
questions.explanation: Speakers receive electrical signals from the sound card and convert them into audible sound through the vibration of speaker cones.

questions.question: Which output device is best suited for producing architectural drawings?
questions.explanation: Plotters are ideal for large-format, high-precision drawings such as architectural plans, engineering blueprints, and ship designs.

questions.question: Chain printers are capable of printing at speeds of:
questions.explanation: Chain printers use a continuously moving metallic chain with characters and can print at 400–2500 lines per minute.

topics.title: Software: System and Application
topics.description: Understanding the differences between system software and application software, with examples and use cases.

questions.question: Software is best defined as:
options: A set of instructions that tell the computer what to do
questions.explanation: Software is a set of instructions that tells the computer what to do, how to do it, and what type of results to produce.

questions.question: System software is designed to:
options: Manage computer hardware and provide a platform for application software
questions.explanation: System software manages the computer's hardware resources and provides the platform (environment) on which application programs run.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of system software?
questions.explanation: Windows is a system software (operating system) that manages hardware and provides a platform for running applications like MS Word or Chrome.

questions.question: Application software is designed to:
questions.explanation: Application software is created for users to perform specific tasks like word processing, accounting, image editing, or browsing.

questions.question: Microsoft Office is an example of a:
questions.explanation: Microsoft Office is a software suite — a collection of related but independent programs (Word, Excel, PowerPoint) with a shared interface.

questions.question: Which of the following is utility software?
questions.explanation: Utility software performs system maintenance tasks. A disk formatter is a utility, while Photoshop, Media Player, and Google Docs are applications.

questions.question: A Database Management System (DBMS) is categorized as:
questions.explanation: A DBMS is a software system — a collection of fundamental programs that provide database services to a variety of other applications.

questions.question: Which of the following is a software package?
questions.explanation: A software package is a small collection of closely related programs that work together for a task, like a spreadsheet and text processing system.

questions.question: Device drivers are used to:
options: Allow hardware to communicate with the operating system
questions.explanation: Device drivers are system software that enable hardware components (printers, graphics cards, etc.) to communicate properly with the OS.

questions.question: Which type of software would you use to format a hard disk?
questions.explanation: Disk formatting is performed by utility software, which handles system maintenance tasks such as disk management.

questions.question: The operating system belongs to which category of software?
questions.explanation: An operating system (Windows, macOS, Linux) is system software that manages all hardware resources and provides a user interface.

questions.question: An image viewer on its own is an example of:
questions.explanation: A standalone image viewer is a single-program application — the simplest type of application software designed for one specific task.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the difference between system software and application software?
options: System software is bought, application software is free
System software manages hardware; application software performs user tasks
Application software boots the computer; system software entertains users
questions.explanation: System software manages the computer's core functions and hardware, while application software helps users accomplish specific real-world tasks.

questions.question: Which type of software includes text editors and authentication tools?
questions.explanation: Text editors and authentication (login) tools are utility programs — part of system software that maintains the computer environment.

questions.question: Linux is an example of:
questions.explanation: Linux is an open-source operating system — a type of system software that manages hardware and provides a computing environment.

topics.title: Data, Information, Knowledge, and Wisdom
topics.description: Understanding the hierarchy from raw data to wisdom, and how each level of abstraction adds meaning and utility.

questions.question: Data is best defined as:
questions.explanation: Data consists of raw, unprocessed facts and figures that have no specific context or meaning on their own.

questions.question: Information is obtained when data is:
questions.explanation: For data to become information, it must be processed — contextualized, categorized, calculated, and condensed — to convey meaning.

questions.question: Information answers questions that begin with:
questions.explanation: Information provides answers to 'who, what, where, when, and how many' questions, giving data relevance and purpose.

questions.question: Knowledge is best described as:
questions.explanation: Knowledge is the application of information; it answers 'how' questions and involves understanding derived from experience.

questions.question: Wisdom in the data-information-knowledge hierarchy refers to:
questions.explanation: Wisdom is the highest level — evaluated understanding that allows one to make sound judgments and decisions based on accumulated knowledge.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly represents the hierarchy from lowest to highest?
questions.explanation: The hierarchy is: Data (raw symbols) → Information (processed data) → Knowledge (applied information) → Wisdom (evaluated understanding).

questions.question: A list of 500 random numbers is an example of:
questions.explanation: A list of uncontextualized numbers is raw data — it has no meaning or relevance until it is processed and given context.

questions.question: A sales report showing revenue trends for Q3 is an example of:
questions.explanation: A sales report is information — data that has been processed, contextualized, and organized to convey a meaningful trend.

questions.question: A doctor using patient symptoms to determine the best treatment plan is applying:
questions.explanation: Using information (patient symptoms) and experience to determine how to treat — answering 'how' — is the application of knowledge.

questions.question: Processing data produces:
questions.explanation: When data is processed (organized, categorized, calculated), it becomes information — data with context and meaning.

questions.question: The content of the human mind is classified into four categories which are:
questions.explanation: The four cognitive levels are: Data (symbols), Information (processed data), Knowledge (applied information), and Wisdom (evaluated understanding).

questions.question: Which level of the data hierarchy answers 'how' questions?
questions.explanation: Knowledge answers 'how' questions — it represents the understanding of how to apply information to solve problems.

questions.question: Raw symbols and facts without context are known as:
questions.explanation: Data consists of raw symbols and facts with no inherent context or organization. It is the most basic level of the hierarchy.

questions.question: Which transition is correctly described?
questions.explanation: Processing data produces information, and processing information produces knowledge — moving up the data-information-knowledge hierarchy.

questions.question: An experienced business leader making strategic decisions based on decades of industry experience is demonstrating:
questions.explanation: Using evaluated understanding accumulated over time to guide sound judgment is the definition of wisdom — the highest level of the hierarchy.

topics.title: Computer Viruses, Malware, and Internet Security
topics.description: Types of malware including viruses, worms, Trojan horses, spyware, and best practices for prevention and internet security.

questions.question: A Trojan horse is best described as:
options: A program that appears harmless but performs malicious actions
questions.explanation: A Trojan horse is a malicious program disguised as something harmless (like a game); when run, it causes damage such as deleting files, but does not self-replicate.

questions.question: A key difference between a Trojan horse and a worm is that:
options: A Trojan cannot replicate automatically while a worm can
questions.explanation: Trojan horses do not replicate on their own; they rely on users to run them. Worms, however, automatically copy themselves across networks.

questions.question: A worm spreads by:
options: Exploiting security holes in networks to replicate and spread automatically
questions.explanation: A worm scans networks for machines with specific security holes, copies itself there, and continues replicating — all without user interaction.

questions.question: Which malware type records keystrokes made by a user?
questions.explanation: A keylogger secretly records every key pressed on the keyboard, often used to steal passwords and sensitive personal information.

questions.question: Ransomware is a type of malware that:
options: Locks or encrypts a victim's data and demands payment for release
questions.explanation: Ransomware encrypts the victim's files and demands a ransom payment (often cryptocurrency) in exchange for the decryption key.

questions.question: Spyware is primarily used to:
options: Monitor and collect user activities without consent
questions.explanation: Spyware silently monitors user activities, collecting personal information, browsing habits, or sensitive data to send to unauthorized parties.

questions.question: Adware is malware that mainly:
options: Displays unwanted advertisements on a user's screen
questions.explanation: Adware is software that automatically displays or downloads advertising material, often without the user's explicit consent.

questions.question: To protect against viruses, you should:
options: Install antivirus software and keep it up-to-date
questions.explanation: Installing reputable antivirus software and regularly updating it provides real-time protection against known and new virus threats.

questions.question: An 'on-access' scanner in antivirus software works by:
options: Scanning only when you request it
Checking files each time they are executed, starting with the OS
questions.explanation: On-access scanners (real-time protection) automatically check files and programs each time they are executed, starting with OS boot.

questions.question: Which practice is recommended when downloading software online?
options: Trust all files from chat rooms
Use a virus scan on any new program before executing it
questions.explanation: Always scan new programs or files — whether downloaded, purchased, or received via email — with antivirus software before running them.

questions.question: Why is data backup important in the context of viruses?
options: It allows virus removal without losing data
questions.explanation: Regular backups ensure that if a virus corrupts or deletes files, you can restore your data without permanent loss.

questions.question: Malware is a general term for:
options: Any malicious software designed to harm computer systems
questions.explanation: Malware (malicious software) is a broad category encompassing viruses, worms, Trojans, spyware, ransomware, and other harmful programs.

questions.question: A firewall is used to:
options: Block unauthorized network access and filter traffic
questions.explanation: A firewall monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on security rules, blocking unauthorized access attempts.

questions.question: Phishing attacks aim to:
options: Trick users into revealing personal or financial information
questions.explanation: Phishing involves fraudulent emails or websites that trick users into entering passwords, credit card numbers, or other sensitive data.

questions.question: DoS stands for:
questions.explanation: DoS stands for Denial of Service, an attack that floods a system or network with traffic to overwhelm and crash it.

questions.question: Two-factor authentication provides:
options: An extra security layer beyond just a password
questions.explanation: Two-factor authentication requires two forms of verification (e.g., password + OTP), significantly improving account security.

questions.question: Botnets are networks of:
options: Infected computers controlled by an attacker
questions.explanation: A botnet is a network of infected computers (bots) controlled remotely by an attacker, often used for spam, DDoS attacks, or data theft.

questions.question: Which of the following is a safe online practice?
options: Using strong, unique passwords and updating antivirus regularly
questions.explanation: Safe online practices include using strong passwords, keeping antivirus updated, avoiding suspicious links, and not sharing credentials.

questions.question: A secure website URL typically begins with:
questions.explanation: HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) indicates the website uses SSL/TLS encryption to protect data transmitted between browser and server.

questions.question: Being cautious in online chat rooms is important because:
options: Files and links shared in chat rooms may contain malware
questions.explanation: Online chat rooms can be a source of malicious files and links. Always verify the source before clicking links or downloading files.

topics.title: Computer Classification and Types
topics.description: Classification of computers by size and power including mainframes, minicomputers, microcomputers, laptops, tablets, and smartphones.

questions.question: Mainframe computers are best known for:
options: Processing massive data and handling billions of transactions simultaneously
questions.explanation: Mainframes are large, powerful computers used by major organizations for high-volume data processing, critical applications, and financial transactions.

questions.question: A minicomputer is best described as:
options: Smaller and less expensive than a mainframe but more powerful than a PC
questions.explanation: Minicomputers fill the gap between powerful mainframes and less capable microcomputers, used in labs, small organizations, and education.

questions.question: Microcomputers are built around:
options: A single-chip microprocessor (CPU) integrated with RAM/ROM on a circuit board
questions.explanation: Microcomputers use a single-chip microprocessor that integrates CPU, memory, and I/O on a circuit board — the basis for PCs, laptops, and smartphones.

questions.question: Which type of computer is designed for a single user with high processing power?
questions.explanation: A workstation is a high-performance single-user computer with a powerful microprocessor and high-quality monitor, used for engineering and design.

questions.question: A laptop is best described as:
options: A battery or AC-powered portable personal computer
questions.explanation: A laptop is a portable personal computer that runs on battery or AC power and generally offers the same functionality as a desktop.

questions.question: Netbooks are generally:
options: Cheaper and less powerful but adequate for email and internet
questions.explanation: Netbooks are a type of laptop designed for even greater portability; they are cheaper and less powerful but sufficient for basic internet tasks.

questions.question: Tablets differ from laptops mainly because:
options: Tablets use touch-sensitive screens instead of physical keyboards
questions.explanation: Tablets use a touch-sensitive screen for all interaction, replacing the physical keyboard and mouse found in laptops.

questions.question: Smartphones are best classified as:
questions.explanation: Smartphones are microcomputers — powerful handheld devices built on microprocessor technology that run diverse applications.

questions.question: Mobile devices are designed primarily for:
options: Extreme portability, fitting in a hand or pocket
questions.explanation: Mobile devices (smartphones, tablets, e-readers) are designed to be extremely portable, enabling computing on the go.

questions.question: Which type of computer handles multi-user operations and fills the gap between mainframes and PCs?
questions.explanation: Minicomputers are designed for multi-user operations and fill the capability gap between expensive mainframes and less powerful PCs.

questions.question: Mainframe computers are distinguished from supercomputers by being designed for:
options: High throughput — the rate at which data is processed
questions.explanation: Mainframes are optimized for high throughput (processing large volumes of transactions), while supercomputers are optimized for maximum calculation speed.

questions.question: A personal computer (PC) is:
options: A small, single-user computer based on a microprocessor
questions.explanation: A PC is a small, single-user computer built around a microprocessor, with a keyboard, monitor, and storage — designed for individual use.

questions.question: Which type of computer is optimized for tasks like web browsing, watching videos, and reading e-books?
questions.explanation: Tablet computers are optimized for media consumption tasks such as web browsing, video streaming, reading, and gaming.

questions.question: An e-reader is a type of:
questions.explanation: An e-reader is a type of mobile device — a handheld computing device specifically designed for reading digital books and documents.

questions.question: Computers are generally classified by:
questions.explanation: Computers are generally classified by size and power (though there is considerable overlap): mainframe, mini, micro (including PCs, laptops, phones).

topics.title: Units of Measurement and Number Systems
topics.description: Data size units, CPU speed measurement, and an introduction to number systems used in computing.

questions.question: The smallest unit of data in a computer is:
questions.explanation: A bit (binary digit) is the smallest unit of data, represented as either 0 or 1 in binary computing.

questions.question: One byte is equal to:
questions.explanation: One byte consists of 8 bits and can represent values from 0 to 255 (256 possible states).

questions.question: Approximately how many bytes are needed to store a typical line of text from a book?
questions.explanation: Approximately 90 bytes are sufficient to store a typical line of text from a book.

questions.question: Approximately how much storage does one page of text require?
questions.explanation: A single page of text requires approximately 4 KB (kilobytes) of storage.

questions.question: A typical pocket-book's text requires approximately:
questions.explanation: The text of a typical pocket book requires approximately 120 KB (kilobytes) of storage.

questions.question: A 3-minute song at 128k bitrate occupies approximately:
questions.explanation: A 3-minute MP3 song at 128k bitrate occupies approximately 3 MB (megabytes) of storage.

questions.question: A CD-ROM holds approximately:
questions.explanation: A standard CD-ROM holds between 650 and 900 MB (megabytes) of data.

questions.question: 114 minutes of uncompressed CD-quality audio occupies approximately:
questions.explanation: Approximately 1 GB (gigabyte) is needed for 114 minutes of uncompressed CD-quality audio at 1.4 Mbit/s.

questions.question: A typical flash drive has a storage capacity of:
questions.explanation: Typical flash drives (USB drives) have capacities ranging from 8 to 16 GB, though larger sizes are also common.

questions.question: CPU speed is measured in:
questions.explanation: CPU speed is measured in Hertz (Hz), where 1 Hz represents one CPU cycle per second. Modern CPUs operate in GHz (billions of cycles per second).

questions.question: 1 Hz represents:
questions.explanation: 1 Hz (one Hertz) represents one CPU cycle per second. The speed of a CPU is commonly expressed in GHz (gigahertz = billions of cycles per second).

questions.question: The binary system uses which base?
questions.explanation: The binary system uses base 2, representing all data as combinations of 0 and 1 — the foundation of all digital computing.

questions.question: Digital computers store all information as:
questions.explanation: All information in digital computers — numbers, letters, images, audio — is ultimately stored and processed as binary code (sequences of 0s and 1s).

questions.question: The octal number system uses which base?
questions.explanation: The octal system uses base 8, representing numbers using digits 0 through 7. It is sometimes used as a shorthand for binary.

questions.question: The hexadecimal number system uses base:
questions.explanation: The hexadecimal system uses base 16, with digits 0–9 and letters A–F. It is widely used in computing for memory addresses and color codes.

questions.question: Which number system forms the foundation for all subsequent computer number representations?
questions.explanation: The binary system (base 2) is the foundation of all digital computation; octal, decimal, and hexadecimal are built upon or converted from binary.

questions.question: A bit can hold how many different values?
questions.explanation: A bit is a binary digit that can hold exactly 2 values: 0 (off) or 1 (on), representing the fundamental unit of digital data.

questions.question: One byte can represent a number from 0 to:
questions.explanation: One byte (8 bits) can represent 256 different values (2^8), ranging from 0 to 255.

questions.question: GHz stands for:
questions.explanation: GHz stands for Gigahertz — one billion cycles per second. Modern CPUs typically operate at speeds of 2–5 GHz or higher.

questions.question: Which unit is larger?
questions.explanation: Terabyte (TB) is the largest unit listed. The order from smallest to largest is: KB → MB → GB → TB → PB.

topics.title: Internet Applications and Services
topics.description: Understanding internet services including WWW, email protocols, FTP, VoIP, e-commerce, social media, cloud storage, and online security.

questions.question: The Internet is best described as:
options: A global network of interconnected computers
questions.explanation: The Internet is a massive global network that connects millions of computers and devices worldwide, enabling communication and information sharing.

questions.question: WWW stands for:
questions.explanation: WWW stands for World Wide Web, a system of interlinked hypertext documents accessed via the internet using a web browser.

questions.question: HTTP stands for:
questions.explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the main protocol used to transfer web pages between a web server and a browser.

questions.question: A web browser is used to:
options: Access and display web pages on the internet
questions.explanation: A web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Edge) is software that accesses and renders web pages from the internet for users to view.

questions.question: Which of the following is a web browser?
questions.explanation: Chrome (by Google) is a web browser. Google, Yahoo, and Bing are search engines. Note: Google also owns Chrome, but the question tests browser identification.

questions.question: A website address is also called a:
questions.explanation: A URL (Uniform Resource Locator) is the web address used to access a specific website or resource on the internet.

questions.question: FTP stands for:
questions.explanation: FTP (File Transfer Protocol) is used to transfer files between computers over a network, enabling file uploads and downloads.

questions.question: A search engine is used to:
options: Find information on the internet using keywords
questions.explanation: A search engine (Google, Bing, Yahoo) indexes web content and allows users to search for information using keywords.

questions.question: E-commerce refers to:
options: Buying and selling goods and services over the internet
questions.explanation: E-commerce (electronic commerce) encompasses all buying, selling, and business transactions conducted over the internet.

questions.question: VoIP stands for:
questions.explanation: VoIP (Voice over Internet Protocol) enables voice calls to be made over the internet rather than traditional telephone networks.

questions.question: Telnet is an internet service that allows:
options: Remote login to another computer over the internet
questions.explanation: Telnet is a protocol that allows a user to remotely access and control another computer over the internet via a command-line interface.

questions.question: Cloud storage services allow users to:
options: Store files online and access them from any device
questions.explanation: Cloud storage (Google Drive, Dropbox) allows users to store files on remote servers and access them from any internet-connected device.

questions.question: Google Drive is an example of:
questions.explanation: Google Drive is a cloud storage service provided by Google, allowing users to store, share, and collaborate on files online.

questions.question: SMTP is mainly used for:
questions.explanation: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is the standard protocol for sending outgoing email messages from a client to a mail server.

questions.question: POP3 is used for:
options: Receiving emails from a mail server
questions.explanation: POP3 (Post Office Protocol version 3) is used to retrieve (receive) emails from a mail server to a local email client.

questions.question: IMAP stands for:
questions.explanation: IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol) allows email to be accessed and managed directly on the mail server from multiple devices.

questions.question: Social media platforms are primarily used for:
questions.explanation: Social media platforms (Facebook, Twitter, Instagram) enable social networking, content sharing, communication, and community building.

questions.question: E-learning refers to:
options: Online education delivered through digital platforms
questions.explanation: E-learning is the delivery of education through digital technologies — online courses, video lectures, and interactive platforms like Moodle.

questions.question: Which application is used for online video conferencing and meetings?
questions.explanation: Zoom is a video conferencing application that enables virtual meetings, webinars, and online classes with audio and video capabilities.

questions.question: Cookies in internet browsing are used to:
options: Store user preferences and session information
questions.explanation: Cookies are small text files stored by websites on a user's device to remember preferences, login status, and other browsing information.

questions.question: Spam refers to:
options: Unsolicited bulk email messages sent to many recipients
questions.explanation: Spam is unwanted, unsolicited bulk email (or messages) often used for advertising, phishing, or distributing malware.

questions.question: Which internet service enables file sharing between computers?
questions.explanation: FTP (File Transfer Protocol) is specifically designed for transferring files between computers over a network.

questions.question: Internet banking allows users to:
options: Access bank services, make transfers, and view statements online
questions.explanation: Internet banking enables customers to access account information, make transfers, pay bills, and manage finances via a secure online portal.

questions.question: Encryption is used during internet transmission to:
options: Protect data from unauthorized access by encoding it
questions.explanation: Encryption converts data into a coded format during transmission, ensuring that only authorized parties with the decryption key can read it.

questions.question: A disadvantage of social media is:
questions.explanation: While social media enables communication and sharing, it also creates significant privacy risks through data collection, oversharing, and cyberbullying.
